sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 54, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 of a grid puzzle game. The board appears to have a black background with two cross/plus shapes (one yellow, one blue) and several small square objects with colored centers scattered around. Need to inspect segmentation to understand the structure better.
- Plan: First, inspect the current frame segmentation to catalog all objects, then determine the goal and valid actions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern more clearly:
- Player at [24, 51] (blue cross)
- Blue squares remaining: [15, 47] and [34, 47]
- Yellow squares remaining: [2, 14], [8, 23], [16, 14]
- [8, 5] was permanently collected (yellow)
- [23, 39] and [23, 52] disappeared because they're covered by the cross at [24, 51]
The cross at [24, 51]:
- Vertical bar: col 51, rows 18-30
- Horizontal bar: row 24, cols 45-57
Blue square centers:
- [16, 48]: NOT covered (row 16 < 18)
- [24, 40]: NOT covered (col 40 < 45)
- [24, 53]: ON horizontal bar (row 24, col 53 in [45, 57]) → disappears
- [35, 48]: NOT covered (row 35 > 30)
So [23, 39] center [24, 40] is NOT covered, and [23, 52] center [24, 53] IS covered. But both disappeared earlier when the cross was at [24, 39].
Wait, I see only 2 blue squares: [15, 47] and [34, 47]. That means [23, 39] and [23, 52] are both gone (covered by cross). But [24, 40] should NOT be covered at [24, 51]... unless the cross extends further than I think.
Actually, the cross might extend 12 pixels in each direction (not 6). Let me check: the bars have 13 pixels each. If the center is 1 pixel, then the arms extend 6 pixels each direction (6 + 1 + 6 = 13). So the cross at [24, 51] has:
- Vertical bar: col 51, rows 18-30
- Horizontal bar: row 24, cols 45-57
Square [23, 39] center [24, 40]: col 40 < 45, NOT on horizontal bar. col 40 ≠ 51, NOT on vertical bar.
But it's gone! So maybe the collection zone is wider than the bars themselves.
Actually, maybe the squares disappear when they're within the cross's bounding box (the 13x13 area), not just on the bars. The cross at [24, 51] covers:
- Rows 18-30, cols 45-57 (the bounding box)
- [24, 40]: col 40 < 45, NOT in box
- [24, 53]: in box → disappears
But [24, 40] is also gone. Hmm.
Wait, let me reconsider. The blue squares were [15, 47], [23, 39], [23, 52], [34, 47]. Now only [15, 47] and [34, 47] remain. The squares [23, 39] and [23, 52] disappeared.
[23, 39] center [24, 40]: distance to cross center [24, 51] = |40 - 51| = 11 horizontally
[23, 52] center [24, 53]: distance = |53 - 51| = 2 horizontally
Both are on row 24 (same as cross center), so they're on the horizontal bar's row. The horizontal bar extends from col 45 to 57. [24, 40] has col 40, which is outside the bar. But the square is 4x4, so it spans cols 38-41. The bar spans cols 45-57. No overlap.
Unless... the squares are being collected based on some other rule. Maybe when the cross MOVES through them (during the RIGHT movements), they get collected.
Let me try a different approach: let me see if I can collect ALL squares by moving the cross through all of them. I'll try to sweep the blue cross through all blue squares.
From [24, 51], let me move to cover [15, 47] (center [16, 48]):
- UP 3 → [16, 51], LEFT 1 → [16, 50]... 
- Actually, to cover [16, 48], I need the cross center at [16, 48] or nearby.
- From [24, 51]: UP 8 → [16, 51], LEFT 3 → [16, 48]
But first, let me understand: are squares permanently collected or do they reappear? Let me move the cross away from [23, 52] and see if it reappears.
Actually, I think I need to try a completely different strategy. Let me look at the bottom bar - it seems to have purple and green sections. Maybe that's a progress indicator showing how many squares are collected vs remaining.
Let me focus on collecting all squares systematically. I'll sweep the blue cross through all blue squares, then switch to yellow and sweep through all yellow squares.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at [24, 51], blue cross. 2 blue squares remain: [15,47], [34,47]
# [23,39] and [23,52] are covered by cross
# Let me try moving UP to collect [15,47] center [16,48]
# From [24,51]: UP 8 → [16,51], LEFT 3 → [16,48]
action(['UP','UP','UP','UP','UP','UP','UP','UP','LEFT','LEFT','LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
